misconceptionSummary: Students think subtraction always makes a number smaller.

visuals.ruleText: Addition moves right on the number line; subtraction moves left
visuals.subText: Subtracting a negative moves RIGHT — it makes the number bigger

steps.narration: Addition always moves right; subtraction always moves left on the number line. Subtracting a positive moves left — the number decreases. But subtracting a negative reverses direction and increases the value.

lines.text: Adding zero changes nothing: a + 0 = a
Subtracting zero changes nothing: a − 0 = a
Zero is the identity element for addition

steps.narration: Zero is the identity for addition and subtraction. Adding or subtracting zero leaves any number exactly where it started. This sounds obvious but underpins a lot of algebraic reasoning.

lines.text: Subtraction is addition of the opposite

steps.narration: Every subtraction can be rewritten as adding the negative. Ten minus three is the same as ten plus negative three. This unifies addition and subtraction into one operation.

visuals.label: Subtraction = adding the opposite; zero is the identity

steps.narration: Subtraction is addition of the negative. That one idea explains why subtracting a negative makes a number bigger — you are adding its positive opposite.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking subtraction always decreases a number. Subtracting a negative increases it — 5 − (−3) = 5 + 3 = 8.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Subtraction moves left on the number line — unless you are subtracting a negative, which reverses direction. Subtracting a negative is the same as adding a positive.
